item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shaggy made me fear the sun, Rounder - the fog, and now, you make me fear the rain. Wonderfully written.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent series of edits was reverted as many of them were stylistic changes, and as such require permission from the original author prior to such changes being made.
we did it folks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it slightly difficult to believe that a young person would keep a journal of this quality and detail throughout all that was happening, but I was so thoroughly captivated by the characterisation and just plain horror through this that it's a concern I can easily look past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it seems that when it's not an SCP-3300 event, the citizens are basically human. what if someone from outside the town comes in and has a kid with one of them before the next event? will the kid be subject to the effects too?

item.title: Re: OH SHIT I FORGOT TO DO AN AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded: Turns out mnesitcs are just adderall.

item.title: This is my all time favorite SCP
item.content:encoded: I love the direction an narrative of the story, I didn't really understand the "dream" parts but it was still very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Forget to comment on this back during 3k: this skip has amazing imagery, and the personality of all the characters shines through crystal-clear. Reads like an actual novel, and probably one of the best skips on the wiki when it comes to creating a fleshed-out story in a (relatively) short amount of space.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I bless the rains down in Africa… No laughs? Okay fine.
I love this skip, and the journal entries are downright terrifying the more I think about it. You've earned your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what's going on, but I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about an SCP that sucks up hydration, which clears the sky of any clouds? And envelopes increasingly large amounts of hydration until the planet would eventually be sucked dry?

Dunno, something I just came up with. Wouldn't mind someone taking it somewhere though.

item.title: Legal Issues
item.content:encoded: So, does that mean that since the one healthy kid brought the magic water stuff, and the town members weren't water affected by the SCP before, would they be adopted children? The rain called them their children, but they weren't literally their children, as the main character lived with her mom and they had no previous memory of bleeding water before. Also, how did the foundation get ahold of the main characters Diary? Do the people reset only, or is it the entire town? What's up with the sentient rain thing? Are you saying that the entire water cycle is built off of these people turning into water, or is it just for the temporal anomaly of that specific town as a way to explain their dissapearences? It was a good article, but it kinda seemed more like a storybook Foundation Tale/Creepy Pasta than article once it got to the Addendum imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Water way to go, am I right? Seriously, great work blending the imagery of the water cycle with surprisingly disturbing body horror. This gets an easy +1 from me.

item.title: Awesome!
item.content:encoded: I personally love this one, especially reading the journal entries. It made me sad when they were done. I think you should expand on more of the past citizens, maybe have Foundation operatives find more journals, or other media. Just a thought, great overall!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Outside citizens familiar with disappeared instances of SCP-3300 will not comment on their absence unless reminded

Shouldn't this say SCP-3300-1?

item.title: Re: OH SHIT I FORGOT TO DO AN AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded:
In your first section discussing containment, you're using a Class Y "mnestic" - I think your word got guillotined.

"Class Y" is usually a mnestic in prior usage and it also doesn't make sense that the Foundation would dose their own people with weekly amnestics in this case, because not remembering anything would be pretty useless. On the other hand, I don't get why mnestics are that useful here either because:

This effect affects focus rather than memory

item.title: Re: OH SHIT I FORGOT TO DO AN AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded:
You did an incredible job blending the surreality/body horror with the psychological/existential horror in a way where it doesn't feel overused or cliche. The events in the journal do a great job of describing the emotional experience involved without downplaying or exaggerating anything - it reads as if a genuine journal. The evaporation of the bodies after death is an excellent touch, especially with them not simply evaporating but boiling.

My only comment constructively comes from that journal in regards to bleeding water - I would think they'd catch on at some point earlier if they don't bleed, since someone is inevitably going to injure themselves and wonder where the hell the red stuff is.

In your first section discussing containment, you're using a Class Y "mnestic" - I think your word got guillotined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… This is actually really good. Considering some of the earlier Series 4 I've read it's an improvement and it actually worked well. A +1 from me.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Dr A Pancake, this post is spammy and not particularly coherent. Please endeavor to refrain from posting such comments in the future; this is not the first spammy comment you have posted thus far.

item.title: oh no rain
item.content:encoded: its raining we all die now yay :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was reading this at break and right afterward we got a severe thunderstorm warning. Boss came in and said we could leave a little early.

Way too spooky. Excellent work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty late (and probably completely uncalled for) for me to be piling on the discussion around this, but…

+ show block
– hide block

I'm inclined to side with the author about this. Granted, I've done little but lurk for as long as I can remember, but I thought critiques were meant to be on the quality of the work, its tone, or general direction - downvoting for something between the lines that wasn't deliberately (much less maliciously) meant to be there, and that you'd acknowledged wasn't, seems about as arbitrary as you possibly can get without sinking to name-calling.

Disregarding the defensiveness of rumetzen's "no u" response, I'd have thought that the whole "fear of the other overpowering/destroying what we know" is as politically charged here as it is in your own (personally better imho, but for completely different reasons) piece - that is to say, not very much.

If anything, I'd say the themes in 3300 are a riposte to anti-refugee propaganda (for the same reasons as a few other replies to this, too) - that at the end of the day, the current residents and new arrivals are exactly the same, and whatever they choose to claim as theirs will end up washed away too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting horror piece on the ebb and flow of water in the water cycle.

And can someone explain to me the anti-refugee sentiments people like Scantron are having? I don't get it since the book he linked to involves white people claiming land but that doesn't link to the people waiting outside the store at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is very interesting but that journal is what made me love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the game Siren (not in a "you ripped this off" way, mind). Both have rain as a central theme, and involve a town becoming some sort of inescapable alternate reality. I liked it, though admittedly I'm a bit fuzzy on what the dreams have to do with anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, it looks like nobody's yet changed "3000" to "3300" since the contest has ended…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's raining outside but that's not unusual
But the way that I'm feeling is becoming usual
I guess you could say
The clouds are moving away
Away from your days
And into mine

Upvoted. Great horror story, and the imagery stays with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta say, I disagree with this. It does in fact read pretty close to those tropes, and Scantron even linked an example. You not being aware of those tropes doesn't mean he's lying our out to get you, or that he needs to do "self-crit" (unclear on the point of the personal attack there, too).

That said, it didn't interfere with my enjoyment of the piece (and FWIW it didn't occur to me until Scantron pointed it out, though now I can't unsee it). And yeah it's obvious that wasn't the authorial intent. I assume that's why Scantron explicitly said "right place, wrong time" for his personal reaction to the piece.

The author not knowing something doesn't mean the audience is full of shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This starts good and gets better. The ending parts are particularly great. The strangers standing outside the house, and the water reveal — excellent. The spooky tone is very good. (I wasn't actually scared by this, but I didn't feel I needed to be.)

Tiniest of nitpicks: You should have a paragraph break after either the first or second paragraph of the description. I had to reread a couple times there before I figured out what was stopping me.

Also a tiny nitpick:

Weird, right? No idea what to make of it.

Too on the nose. Feels like a wink to the audience. (Technically the dialogue line about salvation is on the nose, but it's easier to accept — I didn't really notice until I was paying attention to the bit that I quoted.)

The bit written in the van has way too many words to be believable as written in a panic.

…That technically also applies to the rest of it, but through the rest of it, I didn't mind the trope. So my recommendation is just to tweak the first parts in the van, and then you can get away with the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does that only happen during the replacement event?

Yup.

If so, has the Foundation tried observing a corpse located outside the city during an event?

From the article, "Instances of SCP-3000-1 outside of the town when SCP-3000 occurs will disappear several days after the event begins." This includes dead instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fantasticly spooky article and I can't stop thinking about the nature of this water hive-mind entity. Are the instances being reincarnated? Is the water constantly assimilating new souls (drowning victims?) to replace them with? Are the instances all entirely artificial constructs and the people of the dream village were just used as a template? Such wonderful mystery.

However, I found myself distracted by an apparent contradiction. The description states that post-mortem examination reveals nothing unusual, but the journal describes people evaporating upon death. Does that only happen during the replacement event? If so, has the Foundation tried observing a corpse located outside the city during an event? Or does the cognitohazard effect prevent them from thinking of that idea or from noticing the increased humidity in their morgue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this skip. Especially good since I generally don't like wordy ones, but this is great. It doesn't quite give me the fear that it should in this competition though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really creepy. I like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Margaret sure writes eloquently despite being put into multiple high-stress situations in such a short amount of time. But my disbelief remains suspended.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3000 is accompanied by a moderate cognitohazardous effect. Outside citizens learning about the existence of the town of Clear Water or its citizens will give little thought to them unless the subject is brought to their direct attention. This effect affects focus rather than memory— those affected don’t forget that Clear Water exists, but rather show difficulty paying attention to it. Outside citizens familiar with disappeared instances of SCP-3000 will not comment on their absence unless reminded, and even then show little concern.

"It doesn't look like anything to me."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that is definitely a bit of artistic liberty. I imagine she's writing it during the downtime between incidents. I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You handled the existential horror really well. My only complaint is the narrator's improbable journal keeping. "I grabbed the journal without thinking about it" seems like a hand wave, but everything else worked well enough for me to not think about that until a good half hour after I got done reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the implication is that it was his house, in a previous iteration of the rain. So I think, an even better way is to include a notion of avatars carrying memories of past incarnations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clear Water, Montana

Sure it is. Sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well hey, that was a great article right there.

As I began reading through the addendum I really felt the story grip me and not let go until I had reached the end. The imagery conveyed therein is wonderfully creepy, and I really enjoy the character of Margaret Lane for the brief time we get to know her.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well this was a treat. I've read damn near the entire archive, and for the first time, an SCP has appeared that include story beats that mirror personal experiences. Namely:

- Hanging out at a friend's place, playing D&D while a violent storm (Hurricane Charlie) crashes down around us. Spooky murderous strangers didn't show up, but a tree did spear cleanly through the living room ceiling (missing the cat by like a foot), so close enough.

- Driving through (the aftermath of) another violent storm (Hurricane Katrina) and (nearly) running out of gas. Poor planning made for a rather surreal night.

The SCP earned my upvote on it's own, but this is just gravy.

People are comparing this to "Day Breaks" entry, but thematically I find it a lot more similar to "Anantashesha", in that the horror is derived from the proposed meaninglessness of human experience — maybe even more effectively, considering there's no "monster" other than the imagery used to depict the people that will come to occupy the space you do now, sparing no thought of your passing.

On that note, and with regards to the discussion in the thread here: rumetzen, if you're mulling over ways to avert the "anti-refugee" interpretation (which I didn't get in the slightest, but can't blame anyone for being sensitive in our political climate): write the actions of the strangers to be less overtly violent. Maybe have them just kill with a seemingly innocuous touch, as opposed to a golf club. That may or may not help, but that's my two cents on the the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that you picked a relatively dry state too

I see what you did there ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I resisted so fucking long on putting collapsibles in mine, too. They're just an aesthetic thing. I sold out eventually… gave in to the man…

Also, fantastic article.

I love that you picked a relatively dry state too: just where you think you'd be safest from this sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you think about it, rain is kind of like water immigrating. And I think this story reminds us that, just as everybody is descended from immigrants, everybody is a watermonster who can only find escape from the horror in their own self destruction.

I kind of lost the thread on this one but it scares the hell out of me, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fear of the "other" probably isn't even tertiary, it's probably even less important, especially seeing as how everyone in the town is a spooky water clone and one of the main points is how they're all basically the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, cool. Changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case you probably want an en-dash — use "--".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Danke, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression that the two were mostly interchangeable? I've always used hyphens just because of stylistic preference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much better; flipping vote.

Though that hyphen after "memory" should be a colon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second paragraph of the description: "This portion of the event of ends", but this is the only obvious typo I saw after a fairly diligent look through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congrats Scan, you have just set the record for biggest leap of the imagination for 2017.

EDIT: It's curious that your own entry directly plays with the fear of another culture invading and replacing ours with their own, and yet MINE, in which the fear of an "other" is tertiary at best, is the thinly-veiled anti-refugee propaganda. Sounds like you need to do some self-crit comrade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I thought those themes were added deliberately, my commentary would have been much less polite.

As I've described elsewhere, I'm touchy about the potential for horror to play into undesirable social trends, so when a plot point in an SCP mirrors a plot point in The Camp of the Saints, I go with my gut.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: great! I love foreshadowing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what CWW is implying or not, not being them. They seem to just be saying it shares traits or tropes with unpleasant things seen elsewhere.

There are some arguments that horror unconsciously reflects the broader anxieties of society for the given time, often regardless of the views of the creators, although the argument is somewhat clearer for films.

Not saying I saw it, but drawing parallels is an existing approach to critique.
—
On my end, I think the main horror entailed the idea of being wiped from existence without a trace after a year of living normally, with nobody anywhere noticing or caring.

Switching gears to the more tangible, physical account in the journal really moved that emotion into… more traditional, horror movie territory, and really kind of sapped it for me. It just happening was way scarier to me than the fear of some kind of monstrous force out to get me.

No vote, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess strangers coming in and being villainous/taking your home?

Idk it's a massive stretch but it's a better reason than I've seen other people downvote stuff for

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Death of the author is a real thing, and I'm of the opinion that everything is political, I'm just curious why CWW saw "Anti-Refugee" sentiment in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people when reading, see things that weren't put there. For some people it's politics, for others it's religious themes, for still others it's oblique Star Wars references…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How so? Is this some kind of in-joke I don't get?
